did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper developed and evaluated a lightweight Convolutional Neural Network (LWCNN) using a transfer learning strategy to automate the classification of 8 skin lesion types from dermoscopic images in the HAM10000 dataset. The study achieved an accuracy of 0.9889 and F1-score of 0.9888, demonstrating the use of deep learning for dermatological diagnosis.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work where a lightweight CNN (LWCNN) was developed and evaluated on the HAM10000 dataset for skin lesion classification. It reports specific performance metrics (accuracy 0.9889, F1-score 0.9888, AUC 0.9968) and compares the proposed model against state-of-the-art methods, which characterizes it as a research paper rather than a review.

title: Lightweight CNN Towards Skin Lesions Automated Diagnosis In Dermoscopic Images
journal: 2023 International Conference on Innovations in Intelligent Systems and Applications (INISTA)
brief_summary: This study developed a lightweight Convolutional Neural Network (LWCNN) to automate the diagnosis of eight skin lesion types from dermoscopic images. The model was trained and evaluated using the HAM10000 dataset, which contains 10,015 images, and employed Random Over Sampling to address class imbalance. The proposed LWCNN was compared against various transfer learning models in terms of performance, complexity, and inference time. The method achieved an accuracy of 0.9889, an F1-score of 0.9888, and an AUC of 0.9968, with a low parameter count of 1.612 million and an inference time of 6ms/step. The results indicate that the LWCNN is the most performant and least complex model among those tested, demonstrating high potential for clinical use in skin lesion classification.

all_task_types.target: 8 discrete skin lesion classes (Melanocytic Nevi, Benign Keratosis-like lesions, Melanoma, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Actinic Keratoses and Intraepithelial Carcinoma / Bowen's disease, Dermatofibroma, Vascular Lesions, Squamous Cell Carcinoma)
all_task_types.details: The paper automates the diagnosis of skin lesions by classifying dermoscopic images into 8 specific categories using the HAM10000 dataset. The model predicts discrete diagnostic labels (Class IDs 0-7). Performance is evaluated using classification metrics such as Accuracy (0.9889), F1-score (0.9888), AUC (0.9968), Specificity, and Sensitivity. The task is explicitly described as 'classifying 8 skin lesions' and 'skin lesion classification'.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: A large collection of multi-source dermatoscopic images of common pigmented skin lesions, gathered from various populations and captured using different techniques. It is a public dataset used for training machine learning applications in academia.
classes_used: Actinic Keratoses and Intraepithelial Carcinoma / Bowen's disease (AK)

use.training: Used for training the proposed Lightweight CNN (LWCNN) model. The dataset was split into a training/validation set (80% of total) and a test set (20% of total). The training/validation subset was further divided into training (90%) and validation (10%) sets. Random Over Sampling (ROS) was applied to balance the classes, resulting in 6705 samples per class for a total of 53,640 images used in the training process.
use.validation: Used to tune the model's hyperparameters and monitor performance during training. Derived from the 80% subset (specifically the 10% portion of that subset).
use.testing: Used to evaluate the final performance of the model. Derived from the 20% subset of the original dataset.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To address class imbalance in the HAM10000 dataset by balancing the number of samples per class.
To divide the dataset for model training, hyperparameter tuning, and final performance evaluation.
To evaluate the model's classification performance by plotting True Positive Rate against False Positive Rate and calculating the Area Under the Curve (AUC).

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using descriptive metrics such as accuracy, F1-score, AUC, and loss, and compared against state-of-the-art methods based on these metric values without statistical significance testing.

HAM10000.preprocessing: Random Over Sampling (ROS) applied to training/validation sets; Test set used for final evaluation
HAM10000.notes: The proposed LWCNN achieved the lowest inference time (6ms/step) and parameter count (1.612M) among all compared models. While ENetB0 had slightly higher overall accuracy (0.9918) and F1-score (0.9919), LWCNN demonstrated superior efficiency. All classes achieved accuracy > 0.9894 and F1-score > 0.9579. AK, VASC, and SCC classes achieved perfect sensitivity (1.0000).

architecture_used.pretraining: Transfer Learning (TL) models (EfficientNetB0, EfficientNetV2B0, EfficientNetB1, EfficientNetV2B1, MobileNet, MobileNetV2, DenseNet121) were used as feature extractors for comparison; the proposed LWCNN was trained from scratch using a trial-and-error methodology.

architecture_details.structure: Two major groups: Features Extractor (FE) and Classifier.
architecture_details.features_extractor: Three repetitive blocks. Each block contains: 1 Conv 2D, Max Pool 2D. The repetitive blocks within the FE contain: 2 Conv 2D, Max Pool 2D, and Batch Normalization (BN).
architecture_details.classifier: Contains Flatten, Dropout, Dense layers, and 4 repetitive blocks. Each repetitive block contains: Dense and BN layers.
architecture_details.activation_functions: Not explicitly specified for LWCNN, but ReLU and LeakyReLU were mentioned in the context of related works [19].

training_details.learning_rate: 1e-3 for epochs <= 10; lr * e^-0.1 for epochs > 10
training_details.hardware: CPU: 2.20 GHz Intel Xeon; RAM: 30GB; GPU: 16GB NVIDIA T4 x 2

classification_or_output_head.output_activation: Not explicitly specified, but categorical cross-entropy implies softmax output

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: ResNet-50 + DenseNet-201 + DarkNet-53 + DWT-A + LBP+ DA
Vit + multis-cale + Contrastive Learning + DA+ DN + Data Sampling
preprocessing_techniques: Random Over Sampling (ROS) to balance class distribution in the training set
Data splitting into training (90%), validation (10%), and test (20%) subsets

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Used to address class imbalance in the HAM10000 dataset by duplicating minority class samples to match the number of majority class samples (6705 per class), resulting in a total of 53,640 images for training.

conclusions: The proposed Lightweight CNN (LWCNN) achieved high performance in automating skin lesion diagnosis, reaching an accuracy of 0.9889, an F1-score of 0.9888, and an AUC of 0.9968 on the HAM10000 dataset.
The LWCNN model demonstrated superior efficiency compared to state-of-the-art transfer learning models, with the lowest parameter count (1.612 million) and the shortest inference time (6ms/step).
The model exhibited strong class-level performance, with accuracy exceeding 0.9894 and F1-score exceeding 0.9580 for all eight skin lesion classes.

The LWCNN outperformed several state-of-the-art methods in terms of F1-score for specific classes, including Melanocytic Nevi (NV), Melanoma (MEL), Basal Cell Carcinoma (BCC), and Squamous Cell Carcinoma (SCC).

The study confirms the potential of lightweight deep learning models to serve as valuable resources for clinicians, offering a balance between high diagnostic accuracy and low computational complexity.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on the HAM10000 dataset, which is known to have significant population bias (primarily European/Caucasian) and imbalanced class distribution, potentially limiting generalizability to diverse global populations.

Class imbalance was addressed using Random Over Sampling (ROS), which duplicates minority class samples; this technique can lead to overfitting and does not reflect the true prevalence of rare skin lesions in clinical practice.

The dataset was split into 80% for training/validation and 20% for testing, with no mention of external validation on independent datasets or cross-validation strategies, raising concerns about data leakage risks and over-optimistic performance estimates.
The model was trained from scratch using a 'trial-and-error' methodology rather than standardized hyperparameter optimization protocols, which may affect reproducibility.

Performance metrics (Accuracy 0.9889, F1-score 0.9888) are exceptionally high, particularly for minority classes like AK and VASC which achieved perfect sensitivity (1.0) after ROS, suggesting potential data leakage or overfitting to the augmented training data.

The paper does not report statistical significance tests (p-values) or confidence intervals for the performance metrics, making it difficult to assess the robustness of the results compared to state-of-the-art methods.

The inference time (6ms/step) and parameter count (1.612M) are reported on specific hardware (NVIDIA T4 GPU), and deployment concerns regarding real-time performance on edge devices or mobile platforms are not addressed.
The study claims clinical relevance but lacks any discussion on integration into clinical workflows, user studies with dermatologists, or evaluation of diagnostic utility in a real-world setting.
